topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the professor's office felt thick, suffocating. I stared at the stack of highlighted papers spread across the mahogany desk, the accusations underlined in harsh red ink. When Dr. Albright finally spoke, his voice was level, almost clinical, which somehow made the critique sting even more. "This section," he began, tapping a pen against a paragraph, "bears an unmistakable similarity to published work. Explain your process." My jaw tightened. I felt a strange heat rising in my chest, a prickly sensation that made my palms sweat. I wanted to retort, to defend the hours I had poured into that research, but the words caught in my throat. I just looked down at my hands, tracing the grain of the wood, trying to keep the tremor out of my voice.

[story 2]

"Are you suggesting this is deliberate?" I asked, my voice coming out sharper than I intended. I leaned forward, resting my elbows on the table, trying to project an authority that felt utterly absent. The silence that followed was heavy, punctuated only by the faint hum of the fluorescent lights overhead. When the committee members exchanged glances, I saw the flicker of disapproval in their eyes, and a deep knot formed in my stomach. I shifted in my seat, pulling my sweater tighter around myself, a small, involuntary movement betraying the tightness gripping me. I felt a sudden, burning desire to argue the sheer effort involved, the genuine intellectual journey that had led to that submission, but I clamped my mouth shut, forcing my gaze to remain fixed on the center of the room.

[story 3]

The accusation landed like a physical blow. I could feel my cheeks flush, a deep, uncomfortable warmth spreading across my face. When the student representative asked for clarification, I found myself speaking too quickly, my words tumbling over each other in a frantic attempt to regain control of the narrative. "It wasn't copying," I insisted, my tone strained, pitched higher than normal. I could feel my throat constricting, making it difficult to draw a steady breath. Every glance from the panel felt like a spotlight, exposing some perceived failing. I picked up my pen, but instead of signing, I simply gripped it so tightly my knuckles turned white, the pressure a desperate anchor against the rising tide of frustration churning beneath my composure.
